title: Lesson 19: AI Agents & Agentic Workflows Part 19

sections.content: Overview

Advanced agentic workflows represent a sophisticated paradigm shift in how autonomous systems orchestrate multi-step problem-solving across distributed environments. Traditional imperative programming requires developers to explicitly define every execution path, but agentic systems must operate with incomplete information, dynamic goals, and uncertain outcomes in real-world domains. Without robust orchestration mechanisms, agents fail to coordinate complex tasks that require resource allocation, goal refinement, temporal reasoning, and failure recovery across asynchronous operations. The core challenge solved by advanced agentic workflows is enabling agents to decompose nebulous objectives into executable subtasks, maintain execution state across interruptions, reason about causality and preconditions dynamically, and adapt strategies when environmental conditions shift. Production systems at Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic rely on these patterns to build agents that can orchestrate API calls, manage memory hierarchically, handle concurrent operations, and gracefully degrade when constituent services fail—none of which emerge naturally from standard LLM inference loops. This lesson explores the architectural patterns, memory management strategies, and control flow mechanisms that separate prototype chatbots from production-grade autonomous agents capable of operating at scale.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider Rohit Sharma leading the Indian cricket team through a five-day Test match at the MCG against Australia, where the team must chase 450 runs with unpredictable pitch behavior, variable weather, and injuries to key players occurring mid-match. At the start of each day's play (agentic cycle), Rohit must assess the current match state—wickets lost, run rate, available resources (healthy batsmen and bowlers)—then decompose the broader goal (win the Test) into immediate tactical subtasks: which bowler to deploy against each batter, when to shift from defensive to aggressive batting, how to preserve fast bowlers through strategic field placements. Each player (agent component) executes their role—pace bowlers maintain economy rate, batsmen adjust strike rotation, fielders anticipate trajectory—but the captain constantly monitors outcomes against expectations and reallocates resources (bringing in spin bowlers if bounce increases, promoting aggressive batsmen if behind run-rate target). The parallel becomes clear: just as Rohit cannot script an entire Test match in advance because conditions evolve moment-to-moment, an autonomous agent cannot execute a fixed plan—it must sense current environment state, decompose remaining objectives into subtasks with appropriate resource allocation, execute with monitoring, and dynamically replan when failures (dropped catches, unexpected weather delays) occur. This reveals why agentic workflows require hierarchical goal decomposition, real-time state monitoring, resource constraints, and adaptive replanning—precisely mirroring how a great captain orchestrates eleven players toward victory despite inherent uncertainty.
Core Concepts
Goal Decomposition and Hierarchical Task Planning

Goal decomposition is the foundational mechanism by which agentic systems translate abstract, high-level objectives into concrete, executable subtasks organized in hierarchical structures. When an agent receives a goal like 'analyze market trends and generate investment recommendations for technology sector stocks,' it cannot execute this directly—it must recursively decompose it into smaller, verifiable subtasks such as 'fetch historical price data for FAANG stocks,' 'compute technical indicators (RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands),' 'analyze earnings reports for sentiment,' and 'synthesize findings into ranked recommendations.' The decomposition process depends critically on the agent's capability model—understanding which primitive actions it can reliably execute (API calls, database queries, computations) versus which require further subdivision. Hierarchical organization creates dependencies: some tasks are prerequisites (data retrieval must precede analysis), some are parallelizable (fetching multiple price datasets), and some are conditional (advanced analysis only triggers if data quality exceeds threshold). Advanced agentic systems implement this through explicit planning modules that use techniques like Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) planning or LLM-based reasoning to generate task graphs, then schedule execution respecting resource constraints, precedence relationships, and temporal deadlines. The quality of decomposition directly determines agent performance—overly coarse decomposition creates brittle subtasks that fail atomically, while excessive fine-graining creates coordination overhead and exponential state explosion.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Steve Smith, batting for Australia against India's express pace attack in the first innings, receiving the team objective 'post a competitive total exceeding 400 runs to establish match dominance.' Smith cannot execute this single goal—he must hierarchically decompose it: first, assess the pitch characteristics and Bumrah's bowling pattern through initial defensive batting (gather information), then identify scoring opportunities in gaps between fielders (decompose into run-scoring subtasks), later transition to aggressive shot-making against weaker bowlers (conditional subtask triggered by match state), and finally, construct partnerships with lower-order batsmen to accumulate tail-end runs (prerequisite: batsmen must survive Bumrah's hostile spells). Smith's batting strategy creates a task hierarchy: immediate subtask (survive next Bumrah over), intermediate subtask (score 40 runs by next drinks break), and high-level objective (reach 400-run milestone). Each subtask has dependencies—he cannot execute aggressive batting effectively until he gauges the pitch (prerequisite), and he must modify strategy if key partnerships break (replanning triggered by environmental change). This mirrors goal decomposition: an agent translates abstract goals into hierarchical task trees, understands execution dependencies, identifies which subtasks are parallelizable versus sequential, and modifies the tree when outcomes diverge from predictions. The insight is that just as Smith's batting success depends on intelligent task hierarchization reflecting match dynamics, agent performance depends on decomposition quality reflecting both capability boundaries and environmental constraints.
State Management and Contextual Memory Architecture

State management in agentic workflows encompasses the mechanisms by which systems maintain, organize, and selectively retrieve information across extended operation lifecycles. Unlike stateless function calls that process isolated inputs, agents must track execution history, intermediate results, environmental observations, and discovered constraints—often across dozens of subtasks and hours of operation. Production systems implement multi-tier memory architectures: working memory holds the immediate decision context (current goal, available subtasks, recent observations), episodic memory stores timestamped records of actions taken and outcomes observed, semantic memory contains generalized knowledge and learned patterns, and procedural memory encodes reusable strategies and task templates. The complexity emerges from managing context windows—LLM-based agents operate within finite token limits, forcing careful decisions about what information to retain in focus versus archive for retrieval-on-demand. Advanced systems implement dynamic memory compression: summarizing completed task phases to conserve tokens, abstracting away low-level execution details while preserving decision-critical facts, and using retrieval-augmented mechanisms to efficiently query archived state when needed. State consistency becomes critical in distributed agentic systems where multiple agent instances or components operate concurrently—without careful locking or version control, agents risk reading stale observations or duplicating effort. Temporal reasoning requires timestamp-aware state management: understanding causality (task B's failure might retroactively affect task A's relevance), identifying time windows (an opportunity that closed cannot be recovered), and managing deadlines (resource-constrained agents must prioritize time-critical subtasks). The overhead of sophisticated state management is substantial, but necessary for agents operating in production environments where replanning costs and failure recovery complexity demand rich contextual awareness.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the Indian cricket team's dressing room during a grueling five-day Test match at Old Trafford, where the captain, coaches, and support staff must maintain sophisticated state awareness across multiple dimensions—current match scorecard (working memory of immediate game state), detailed records of every delivery bowled and shot played throughout the innings (episodic memory of historical data), general knowledge about weather patterns, pitch behavior trends, and opposing team strengths (semantic memory), and proven strategies for batting against reverse swing or defending bouncy pitches (procedural memory). At the start of each over, the captain retrieves current state: batter's confidence level, runs needed, overs remaining, bowler's fatigue indicators—essentially querying working memory for immediate decision context. After every powerplay segment ends, coaches conduct summary analysis, abstracting away irrelevant details (e.g., discarding full delivery logs of successful overs) while preserving strategic insights (batsmen struggled with short-pitch deliveries from Stokes). Between sessions, the dressing room updates archival records—yesterday's pitch notes become background reference material, retrievable if similar conditions emerge today. Distributed state appears when multiple coaching staff operate concurrently—the batting coach advises the batter on technique while the bowling coach plans the next spell; without coordination (state locking), they might recommend conflicting strategies. Temporal reasoning surfaces constantly: did the pitch deteriorate yesterday (historical observation affecting today's strategy)? When does the match window close for aggressive batting (deadline-aware scheduling)? The parallel crystallizes: just as a cricket team's success depends on maintaining rich, organized, and dynamically managed state across working/episodic/semantic/procedural tiers, agentic systems require hierarchical memory architectures with selective compression, retrieval mechanisms, and temporal awareness to operate effectively across extended sequences.
Adaptive Replanning and Failure Recovery Strategies

Adaptive replanning is the mechanism by which agents detect divergence between expected and actual outcomes, diagnose root causes, and modify execution strategies without manual intervention. In deterministic systems, an unexpected failure typically halts execution; agents must instead treat failures as information—signals that environmental assumptions were violated or capability estimates were inaccurate. The replanning cycle begins with outcome monitoring: as each subtask completes, agents compare results against predictions (e.g., 'expected 50ms latency, observed 500ms latency', or 'expected API call to succeed, received rate-limit error'). When divergence exceeds tolerance thresholds, agents classify the failure type: was it transient (network hiccup—retry with backoff), systemic (service overloaded—shift to alternative service), or capability-based (attempted operation outside agent competence—decompose further or escalate)? Different failure types trigger different recovery strategies. Transient failures call for simple retry loops with exponential backoff to respect service load. Systemic failures require strategy adaptation—shifting to alternative APIs, degrading gracefully to reduced-fidelity solutions, or pausing operations until downstream systems recover. Capability-based failures necessitate fundamental replanning: if a subtask requires human expertise, agents should escalate rather than repeat failed attempts. Advanced systems maintain learned failure profiles—after encountering similar failures previously, agents can skip directly to proven recovery strategies, dramatically reducing mean time to recovery. The replanning algorithm itself often uses iterative deepening: first attempt minimal modifications to the original plan, and only if those fail, perform more aggressive restructuring. In safety-critical domains, replanning includes rollback mechanisms: if an agent discovers a subtask produced incorrect results, it must invalidate downstream subtasks and potentially restart entire phases. The challenge of adaptive replanning scales with environmental complexity—agents operating in domains with high uncertainty and many failure modes require sophisticated probabilistic reasoning about recovery outcomes, not just reactive heuristics.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Envision Virat Kohli batting in a World Cup semi-final against Pakistan at a volatile pitch, where his pre-match plan to 'score 70+ runs via aggressive batting against the spin bowling' encounters unexpected events—the pitch deteriorates faster than forecast (environmental assumption violated), or Pakistan's pace bowlers perform exceptionally, negating his anticipated scoring opportunities. Kohli continuously monitors divergence between expected and actual outcomes: he planned to accumulate runs against Hasan Ali's short-pitch bowling, but observes Ali executing unexpected yorkers with accuracy. This divergence triggers the replanning cycle. Kohli classifies the failure type: is Ali's superior performance temporary (just one good over—retry similar approach), systemic (Ali has fundamentally adjusted his attack—requires strategy shift), or capability-based (Kohli's technique against yorkers is weaker than expected—requires defensive positioning). For transient issues (one erratic over), Kohli applies simple retry logic—stays with aggressive mindset next over. For systemic failures (Ali consistently bowling yorkers), he enacts aggressive replanning: shift to rotating strike, reduce scoring attempts against Ali, target his weaker new-ball partner instead. For capability-based failures (Kohli's edges suggest he's uncomfortable against short-pitched bowling), he triggers fundamental strategy restructuring—extend his stay to build partnership and accumulate runs defensively, shifting the burden to later batsmen. Throughout, Kohli maintains failure memory: 'last time I faced this bowler in 2019, I succeeded by waiting for loose balls'—learned failure profiles that accelerate recovery decisions. If he gets dismissed attempting a risky shot (plan produced failure), the XI must rollback—not repeat the aggressive strategy that cost Kohli's wicket, instead inserting cautious batsmen. This mirrors agentic replanning: agents detect outcome divergence, classify failure root cause, apply appropriate recovery strategies (transient retry, systemic adaptation, capability escalation), leverage historical failure memory, and implement rollback when necessary. The insight is that robust autonomy requires not just good initial planning, but sophisticated failure diagnosis and strategy adaptation reflecting real-world uncertainty.
Resource Allocation and Constraint Satisfaction

Resource allocation in agentic workflows addresses the problem of distributing limited computational, temporal, and financial resources among competing subtasks to maximize overall objective achievement. Agents typically operate under multiple constraints: token budgets for LLM inference (expensive), API call quotas (rate-limited), computation time windows (deadline-driven), and memory limits (physical hardware bounds). Unlike static resource planning, agents must allocate resources dynamically as new information emerges about task complexity, subtask dependencies, and remaining time. The allocation problem is fundamentally a constrained optimization challenge: agents seek task orderings and resource distributions that maximize expected goal satisfaction probability while respecting hard constraints (cannot exceed token budget) and soft constraints (prefer completing time-sensitive tasks first). Advanced systems employ techniques from operations research: priority-based scheduling prioritizes high-impact subtasks when under severe resource constraint, cost-benefit analysis allocates resources to subtasks with highest expected marginal value, and preemptive scheduling halts low-priority tasks if higher-priority ones emerge. Temporal awareness is critical: an agent near a deadline must allocate resources to tasks nearing timeout differently than one with ample time. Inter-task dependencies complicate allocation: if task B depends on task A's output, allocating resources to B before A completes is wasteful. In distributed agentic systems with multiple agents competing for shared resources (API calls to a rate-limited service), allocation becomes game-theoretic—agents must reason about other agents' behavior and coordinate to achieve Pareto-efficient outcomes. The allocation algorithm itself consumes resources, creating a meta-optimization problem: sophisticated allocation algorithms may cost more than the efficiency gains they produce. Production systems typically use hybrid approaches: pre-compute allocation strategies for common scenarios (high confidence goals use aggressive resource allocation), then adapt online as new information emerges.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Picture the Australian cricket team's captain and bowling coach during a T20 innings where they must allocate limited bowling resources—five overs each from three frontline bowlers (Pat Cummins, Josh Hazlewood, Mitchell Starc), plus four overs from a spinner and part-time bowler—to defend a target of 180 against India's aggressive batting lineup, with wickets as the primary success metric. The captain faces constant resource allocation decisions under constraints: each bowler has a fixed quota (5 overs maximum), cumulative team resources are limited (20 overs total), and the deadline is absolute (T20 format ends at 20 overs). Early in India's innings, dynamic information emerges—Rohit Sharma looks vulnerable against short-pitch bowling, Virat Kohli dominates spin, and the pitch favors pace bowlers as it dries. The captain reallocates resources dynamically: seeing Rohit's vulnerability, he front-loads Cummins' overs in early powerplay when Rohit bats, abandoning the pre-match plan to distribute bowling evenly. When Kohli arrives mid-innings, the captain deploys the spinner, consuming spinner quota early because Kohli's appearance changes value calculations. Inter-task dependencies surface: the captain cannot afford to 'waste' Starc's overs early (high-impact resource) if that forces him to deploy weaker bowlers against the tail-end explosive batsmen—the tail's potential damage depends on having elite bowlers available later. If India's run rate accelerates unexpectedly (new information), the captain preempts the plan: he halts conservative bowling allocations, immediately deploying Cummins (highest-impact bowler) despite having planned to rest him for later overs. All decisions reflect Pareto-efficiency: the captain seeks the bowling allocation that maximizes wicket probability while respecting the hard constraint of 5 overs per bowler. The meta-problem appears too—the captain spends mental energy deciding allocation; if he over-analyzes, he loses focus on setting field placements, creating a meta-optimization tradeoff. This mirrors agentic resource allocation: agents must dynamically distribute limited resources (tokens, API calls, time) among competing subtasks while respecting constraints, incorporating new information about task value and dependencies, and managing the allocation algorithm's own overhead. The insight is that truly autonomous agents require sophisticated constraint-aware scheduling, not just good task planning.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Advanced Agentic Workflow for Cricket Match Analysis
Demonstrates goal decomposition, state management, adaptive replanning,
and resource allocation in orchestrating autonomous agent tasks.
"""

import json
import time
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Tuple
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from enum import Enum
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(message)s')
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class FailureType(Enum):
    """Categorizes failure types for adaptive replanning"""
    TRANSIENT = "transient"       # Retry with backoff (network hiccup)
    SYSTEMIC = "systemic"         # Strategy adaptation needed (service overload)
    CAPABILITY = "capability"     # Escalate or replan (beyond agent competence)

class TaskStatus(Enum):
    """Task execution lifecycle states"""
    PENDING = "pending"
    EXECUTING = "executing"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    FAILED = "failed"
    REPLANNING = "replanning"

@dataclass
class CricketMatch:
    """Represents a cricket match state—the environment agents operate within"""
    match_id: str
    teams: Tuple[str, str]  # (batting_team, bowling_team)
    target_runs: int
    current_runs: int = 0
    current_wickets: int = 0
    overs_played: float = 0.0
    overs_remaining: float = 20.0  # T20 format
    last_ball_event: Optional[str] = None  # Observed outcome from last delivery
    pitch_conditions: Dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=lambda: {
        "bounce": 0.5,      # 0-1 scale (0=flat, 1=bouncy)
        "pace_assistance": 0.6,
        "spin_assistance": 0.3
    })
    
    def estimated_required_run_rate(self) -> float:
        """Calculate runs-per-over needed to reach target"""
        runs_needed = max(0, self.target_runs - self.current_runs)
        return runs_needed / max(0.1, self.overs_remaining)
    
    def time_pressure_severity(self) -> float:
        """0-1 scale: 0=ample time, 1=critical deadline"""
        overs_used = 20 - self.overs_remaining
        return min(1.0, overs_used / 20)

@dataclass
class Task:
    """Represents an executable subtask in the agentic workflow"""
    task_id: str
    description: str
    estimated_tokens: int       # LLM token cost estimate
    priority: int               # 1-10 (10=highest priority)
    status: TaskStatus = TaskStatus.PENDING
    dependencies: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    result: Optional[Dict] = None
    failure_count: int = 0
    max_retries: int = 3
    created_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    
    def is_executable(self
sections.completed_tasks:
set) -> bool:
        """Check if all dependencies satisfied"""
        return all(dep in completed_tasks for dep in self.dependencies)

@dataclass
class AgentState:
    """Multi-tier memory architecture for the agent"""
    # Working memory: immediate decision context (small, in-focus)
    current_goal: str
    working_tasks: List[Task] = field(default_factory=list)
    
    # Episodic memory: timestamped history of actions and observations
    action_history: List[Dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    observation_history: List[Dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    
    # Semantic memory: generalized knowledge and learned patterns
    failure_profiles: Dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)  # failure_type -> recovery_strategy
    
    # Procedural memory: reusable strategies and templates
    strategy_templates: Dict[str, List[str]] = field(default_factory=dict)  # scenario -> task_templates
    
    # Resource tracking
    token_budget: int = 5000
    tokens_used: int = 0
    api_calls_remaining: int = 100
    
    def tokens_available(self) -> int:
        return max(0, self.token_budget - self.tokens_used)
    
    def add_observation(self
sections.data:
Dict):
        """Record environmental observation in episodic memory"""
        self.observation_history.append({
            "timestamp": time.time(),
            "event": event,
            "data": data
        })
    
    def add_action(self

Dict):
        """Record executed action in episodic memory"""
        self.action_history.append({
            "timestamp": time.time(),
            "action": action,
            "data": data
        })
    
    def learn_failure_recovery(self
sections.recovery:
str):
        """Update semantic memory with learned recovery strategy"""
        self.failure_profiles[failure_type] = recovery

class TaskPlanner:
    """Decomposes abstract goals into hierarchical task structures"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.task_counter = 0
    
    def decompose_goal(self
sections.match_state:
CricketMatch, 
                      agent_capabilities: List[str]) -> List[Task]:
        """Goal decomposition: abstract goal -> concrete subtasks
        
        Example: 'Analyze match conditions and recommend batting strategy'
        Decomposes into:
        - Fetch pitch analysis (semantic memory)
        - Analyze recent deliveries (episodic memory)
        - Evaluate remaining bowlers (working memory)
        - Generate recommendation (synthesis)
        """
        tasks = []
        
        if "analyze" in goal.lower() and "strategy" in goal.lower():
            # High-level goal: match analysis and strategy
            # Decompose into parallel and sequential subtasks
            
            # Parallel subtasks: can execute simultaneously
            tasks.append(Task(
                task_id=f"task_{self.task_counter}",
                description="Fetch historical pitch reports and analyze conditions",
                estimated_tokens=300,
                priority=8,
                dependencies=[]
            ))
            self.task_counter += 1
            
            tasks.append(Task(
                task_id=f"task_{self.task_counter}",
                description="Analyze last 20 deliveries for bowler patterns",
                estimated_tokens=400,
                priority=8,
                dependencies=[]
            ))
            self.task_counter += 1
            
            tasks.append(Task(
                task_id=f"task_{self.task_counter}",
                description="Evaluate remaining bowler capabilities and fatigue",
                estimated_tokens=250,
                priority=7,
                dependencies=[]
            ))
            self.task_counter += 1
            
            # Sequential subtask: depends on above three completing
            pitch_task_id = tasks[0].task_id
            delivery_task_id = tasks[1].task_id
            bowler_task_id = tasks[2].task_id
            
            tasks.append(Task(
                task_id=f"task_{self.task_counter}",
                description="Synthesize findings into batting strategy recommendation",
                estimated_tokens=500,
                priority=9,
                dependencies=[pitch_task_id, delivery_task_id, bowler_task_id]
            ))
            self.task_counter += 1
        
        return tasks

class FailureDetector:
    """Monitors task outcomes against expectations and classifies failures"""
    
    @staticmethod
    def diagnose_failure(expected_outcome: Dict
sections.actual_outcome:
Dict) -> Tuple[FailureType, str]:
        """Classify failure and generate recovery recommendation"""
        
        # Check for transient failures (can retry)
        if "timeout_error" in str(actual_outcome):
            return FailureType.TRANSIENT, "Apply exponential backoff retry"
        
        # Check for systemic failures (need strategy shift)
        if "rate_limit" in str(actual_outcome) or "service_unavailable" in str(actual_outcome):
            return FailureType.SYSTEMIC, "Shift to alternative API or degrade gracefully"
        
        # Check for capability-based failures (escalate or replan)
        if "unauthorized" in str(actual_outcome) or "capability_exceeded" in str(actual_outcome):
            return FailureType.CAPABILITY, "Escalate to human operator or replan with basic tools"
        
        # Default: treat as transient
        return FailureType.TRANSIENT, "Retry with modified parameters"

class AdaptiveReplanner:
    """Implements adaptive replanning logic when failures occur"""
    
    @staticmethod
    def replan_after_failure(failed_task: Task

sections.task_planner:
TaskPlanner,
                            match_state: CricketMatch) -> List[Task]:
        """Generate new plan after detecting failure
        
        Implements recovery strategies based on failure classification:
        - Transient: increment retry counter, re-queue task
        - Systemic: decompose task differently, shift resources
        - Capability: escalate or decompose into simpler subtasks
        """
        replanned_tasks = []
        
        if failure_type == FailureType.TRANSIENT:
            # Transient failure: simple retry with backoff
            failed_task.failure_count += 1
            if failed_task.failure_count <= failed_task.max_retries:
                failed_task.status = TaskStatus.PENDING
                # Increase token estimate for retry (more careful processing)
                failed_task.estimated_tokens = int(failed_task.estimated_tokens * 1.5)
                replanned_tasks.append(failed_task)
                logger.info(f"Transient failure in {failed_task.task_id}: queuing retry #{failed_task.failure_count}")
            else:
                logger.warning(f"Task {failed_task.task_id} exhausted retries")
        
        elif failure_type == FailureType.SYSTEMIC:
            # Systemic failure: strategy adaptation required
            logger.info(f"Systemic failure in {failed_task.task_id}: adapting strategy")
            
            # Example: if pitch analysis API fails, use cached historical data
            if "pitch" in failed_task.description.lower():
                adapted_task = Task(
                    task_id=f"{failed_task.task_id}_adapted",
                    description="Use cached pitch reports and generic model",
                    estimated_tokens=150,  # Lower cost for degraded approach
                    priority=failed_task.priority,
                    dependencies=failed_task.dependencies
                )
                replanned_tasks.append(adapted_task)
        
        elif failure_type == FailureType.CAPABILITY:
            # Capability failure: decompose further or escalate
            logger.info(f"Capability failure in {failed_task.task_id}: escalating")
            # Escalate to human expert or break into simpler subtasks
            escalation_task = Task(
                task_id=f"{failed_task.task_id}_escalated",
                description=f"ESCALATE: {failed_task.description} requires human expertise",
                estimated_tokens=0,  # Escalation handled outside agent
                priority=10,  # Highest priority
                dependencies=[]
            )
            replanned_tasks.append(escalation_task)
        
        return replanned_tasks

class ResourceAllocator:
    """Allocates limited resources (tokens, API calls, time) among competing tasks"""
    
    @staticmethod
    def prioritize_and_allocate(tasks: List[Task]
sections.agent_state:
AgentState, 
                               match_state: CricketMatch) -> List[Task]:
        """Rank tasks by expected value and allocate resources
        
        Scoring considers:
        - Task priority (explicit rank)
        - Deadline sensitivity (time pressure in match)
        - Dependency chain (prefer unblocking dependent tasks)
        - Resource cost efficiency (tokens per expected value)
        """
        
        # Calculate time pressure factor: increases urgency as overs deplete
        time_pressure = match_state.time_pressure_severity()
        
        # Score each task
        scored_tasks = []
        for task in tasks:
            if task.status != TaskStatus.PENDING:
                continue
            
            # Base score: explicit priority
            score = task.priority * 100
            
            # Boost high-priority tasks under time pressure
            if time_pressure > 0.7 and task.priority >= 8:
                score += 500
            
            # Boost tasks that unblock many dependents
            dependent_count = sum(1 for t in tasks if task.task_id in t.dependencies)
            score += dependent_count * 200
            
            # Penalize high-cost tasks if resources are scarce
            resource_ratio = task.estimated_tokens / max(1, agent_state.tokens_available())
            if resource_ratio > 0.5:  # Task costs >50% of remaining budget
                score *= 0.7  # Reduce attractiveness
            
            scored_tasks.append((score, task))
        
        # Sort by score (descending) and return ranked list
        scored_tasks.sort(key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)
        return [task for _, task in scored_tasks]

class CricketMatchAgent:
    """Orchestrates agentic workflow for cricket match analysis"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.state = AgentState(current_goal="Analyze match and recommend strategy")
        self.planner = TaskPlanner()
        self.replanner = AdaptiveReplanner()
        self.allocator = ResourceAllocator()
        self.failure_detector = FailureDetector()
        self.completed_tasks = set()
        self.failed_tasks = set()
    
    def execute_workflow(self
sections.match_state:
CricketMatch) -> Dict:
        """Execute the full agentic workflow: plan -> allocate -> execute -> monitor -> replan"""
        
        logger.info(f"\n{'='*60}")
        logger.info(f"Starting agentic workflow for match {match_state.match_id}")
        logger.info(f"Target: {match_state.target_runs}

remaining):
GOAL DECOMPOSITION
        # Translate abstract goal into hierarchical task structure
        logger.info("
[STEP 1] Goal Decomposition...")
        tasks = self.planner.decompose_goal(
            goal=self.state.current_goal,
            match_state=match_state,
            agent_capabilities=["data_fetching", "analysis", "synthesis"]
        )
        self.state.working_tasks = tasks
        logger.info(f"Decomposed into {len(tasks)} tasks")
        
        # STEP 2: RESOURCE ALLOCATION
        # Prioritize tasks under resource and time constraints
        logger.info("
[STEP 2] Resource Allocation...")
        prioritized_tasks = self.allocator.prioritize_and_allocate(
            tasks=tasks,
            agent_state=self.state,
            match_state=match_state
        )
        logger.info(f"Task execution order (by priority):")
        for task in prioritized_tasks[:5]:  # Show top 5
            logger.info(f"  - {task.task_id}: {task.description[:50]}... (tokens: {task.estimated_tokens})")
        
        # STEP 3: EXECUTION & MONITORING
        # Execute tasks in priority order, monitor outcomes, detect failures
        logger.info("
[STEP 3] Task Execution & Monitoring...")
        for task in prioritized_tasks:
            # Check dependencies
            if not task.is_executable(self.completed_tasks):
                logger.info(f"Skipping {task.task_id} (dependencies not satisfied)")
                continue
            
            task.status = TaskStatus.EXECUTING
            logger.info(f"Executing {task.task_id}...")
            
            # SIMULATE EXECUTION: mock success/failure outcomes
            task_result, execution_success = self._simulate_task_execution(task, match_state)
            
            if execution_success:
                # SUCCESS: record result and mark complete
                task.status = TaskStatus.COMPLETED
                task.result = task_result
                self.completed_tasks.add(task.task_id)
                self.state.tokens_used += task.estimated_tokens
                self.state.add_observation(f"task_completed", {"task_id": task.task_id, "result": task_result})
                logger.info(f"  ✓ {task.task_id} completed (tokens used: {self.state.tokens_used}/{self.state.token_budget})")
            else:
                # FAILURE: detect type and trigger replanning
                logger.warning(f"  ✗ {task.task_id} FAILED")
                self.failed_tasks.add(task.task_id)
                task.status = TaskStatus.REPLANNING
                
                # Diagnose failure type
                failure_type, recovery_strategy = self.failure_detector.diagnose_failure(
                    expected_outcome={"success": True},
                    actual_outcome={"error": "simulated_failure"}
                )
                
                # Learn failure pattern in semantic memory
                self.state.learn_failure_recovery(failure_type.value, recovery_strategy)
                logger.info(f"  Failure classified as: {failure_type.value}")
                logger.info(f"  Recovery strategy: {recovery_strategy}")
                
                # STEP 4: ADAPTIVE REPLANNING
                logger.info(f"  [STEP 4] Adaptive Replanning for {task.task_id}...")
                replanned = self.replanner.replan_after_failure(
                    failed_task=task,
                    failure_type=failure_type,
                    agent_state=self.state,
                    task_planner=self.planner,
                    match_state=match_state
                )
                
                # Add replanned tasks back to working set
                for new_task in replanned:
                    self.state.working_tasks.append(new_task)
                    logger.info(f"  Added replanned task: {new_task.task_id}")
            
            # Check resource constraints
            if self.state.tokens_available() < 100:
                logger.warning("Token budget nearly exhausted; halting new task execution")
                break
        
        # SYNTHESIS: Aggregate results
        logger.info("\n[STEP 5] Synthesis & Recommendation Generation...")
        recommendations = self._synthesize_results()
        
        return {
            "match_id": match_state.match_id,
            "completed_tasks": len(self.completed_tasks),
            "failed_tasks": len(self.failed_tasks),
            "tokens_used": self.state.tokens_used,
            "recommendations": recommendations,
            "learned_failures": self.state.failure_profiles
        }
    
    def _simulate_task_execution(self, task: Task, match_state: CricketMatch) -> Tuple[Dict, bool]:
        """Simulate task execution with probabilistic success/failure
        
        In production, this would be actual API calls, ML inference, etc.
        """
        time.sleep(0.1)  # Simulate processing time
        
        # Mock results vary by task type
        if "pitch" in task.description.lower():
            result = {"bounce": match_state.pitch_conditions["bounce"],
                     "pace_assistance": match_state.pitch_conditions["pace_assistance"]}
            success = True  # Pitch analysis usually succeeds
        elif "delivery" in task.description.lower():
            result = {"recent_patterns": ["short_pitch", "yorker", "slower_ball"],
                     "bowler_effectiveness": 0.72}
            success = True
        elif "bowler" in task.description.lower():
            result = {"available_bowlers": ["Bumrah", "Shami", "Siraj"],
                     "fatigue_levels": {"Bumrah": 0.6, "Shami": 0.8}}
            success = True
        else:
            result = {"recommendation": "Aggressive batting against depleted bowlers"}
            success = True
        
        return result, success
    
    def _synthesize_results(self) -> Dict:
        """Generate final recommendation from completed tasks"""
        return {
            "strategy": "Play aggressive strokes against short-pitched deliveries",
            "focus_areas": ["Accumulate runs in powerplay", "Target fatigued bowlers"],
            "risk_assessment": "Medium risk given time pressure",
            "confidence": 0.85
        }

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Initialize match state
    match = CricketMatch(
        match_id="IND_vs_PAK_T20_2024",
        teams=("India", "Pakistan"),
        target_runs=180,
        current_runs=95,
        current_wickets=3,
        overs_played=10.0,
        overs_remaining=10.0
    )
    
    # Initialize agent
    agent = CricketMatchAgent()
    
    # Execute agentic workflow
    result = agent.execute_workflow(match)
    
    # Display results
    logger.info(f"\n{'='*60}")
    logger.info("WORKFLOW RESULTS")
    logger.info(f"{'='*60}")
    logger.info(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
    logger.info(f"\nFinal Recommendation: {result['recommendations']['strategy']}")
    logger.info(f"Confidence Level: {result['recommendations']['confidence']:.1%}")
```",

      "content": "The code demonstrates the complete agentic workflow lifecycle across all four core concepts. The `CricketMatchAgent` class orchestrates goal decomposition through the `TaskPlanner`, which converts the abstract objective 'Analyze match and recommend strategy' into a hierarchical task tree with explicit dependencies—some tasks (pitch analysis, delivery pattern analysis, bowler evaluation) execute in parallel as they have no interdependencies, while the final synthesis task depends on all three completing. The agent's multi-tier memory architecture appears as the `AgentState` class: working memory maintains the immediate task set and decision context, episodic memory logs actions and observations with timestamps for historical reasoning, semantic memory stores learned failure recovery strategies, and procedural memory encodes task templates for reuse. Resource allocation is handled by `ResourceAllocator.prioritize_and_allocate()`, which scores tasks considering priority, time pressure (overs remaining), dependency chain depth, and token cost efficiency—crucially, tasks are reranked dynamically as the match state changes. The failure and replanning cycle is illustrated when tasks encounter execution problems: the `FailureDetector` classifies failures (transient, systemic, capability-based) based on outcome divergence, then the `AdaptiveReplanner` generates appropriate recovery strategies—transient failures get simple retries with exponential backoff, systemic failures trigger strategy shifts (e.g., using cached data if APIs fail), and capability-based failures are escalated. The workflow execution loop demonstrates this integration: tasks execute, results are monitored, failures trigger replanning that injects new tasks into the working set, and the agent gracefully degrades when token budgets approach depletion. This mirrors production agent behavior where orchestration, failure recovery, and resource management operate continuously across extended operation timescales.",

      "content": "The runtime execution of advanced agentic workflows involves intricate coordination across multiple architectural layers, each with specific performance and reliability characteristics. At the foundation, task scheduling operates as a dynamic priority queue where tasks are continuously re-sorted based on evolving state—when a critical resource becomes unavailable or time pressure escalates, the scheduler must rapidly reorder execution to maintain deadline compliance. The execution engine implements soft real-time constraints: high-priority tasks receive preferential CPU scheduling, but system fairness prevents starvation of lower-priority work, requiring careful tuning of priority multipliers and preemption thresholds. Memory management is sophisticated: working memory must be compact (fitting within LLM context windows of 8K-200K tokens depending on model), so agents implement aggressive compression strategies like abstractive summarization of episodic records and semantic hashing of observation histories. The semantic memory retrieval subsystem uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) techniques: rather than keeping all historical data in working memory, agents embed observations into vector space, then retrieve semantically similar past experiences when facing new situations—this scales memory to millions of episodes while maintaining O(log n) retrieval latency. Temporal reasoning involves clock management across asynchronous operations—agents must accurately track deadline compliance when subtasks complete at unpredictable intervals, often requiring hierarchical timing wheels to efficiently manage thousands of pending timeouts. Distributed coordination surfaces in multi-agent settings where agents share resources or coordinate across systems: lock-free data structures and event-based coordination prevent bottlenecks, but require careful handling of consistency—agents may operate on slightly stale state if freshness checks are expensive. Failure recovery is intertwined with state consistency: when replanning occurs, the agent must determine which completed tasks' results remain valid (if a downstream assumption changed) and which must be invalidated and recomputed—this requires dependency tracking that explodes combinatorially with task count. The token budget accounting is probabilistic: agents estimate LLM inference costs, but actual costs may exceed estimates, forcing runtime renegotiation of task scope. Error handling across the system requires a hierarchy of retry policies: immediate retries for transient network errors, exponential backoff for rate-limited APIs, circuit breakers to halt requests to failing services, and bulkheads to prevent cascade failures where one service's outage doesn't propagate to others. All of this orchestration carries overhead: the agent's planning and decision-making itself consumes tokens and CPU cycles, so sophisticated systems implement budget reservations (allocate 20% of tokens for overhead) and use faster, cheaper models for planning while reserving expensive frontier models for synthesis.",

At the execution level, the physio manages a priority queue of player needs—Bumrah has a tight hamstring (high priority, critical deadline), a backup opener has mental fatigue (medium priority), and a tail-ender has minor bruising (low priority). As the match evolves, priorities dynamically shift: if Bumrah enters his final spell before retirement, his hamstring management becomes critical; if the backup opener walks out to bat in the next 20 minutes, his mental state suddenly escalates in priority. The coaching staff implements soft real-time constraints: the batting coach must provide advice to the current batter immediately (high priority, low latency), but also cannot neglect preparation of the next three batsmen (lower priority, but must complete before they enter). Memory management mirrors the agent's architecture: the batting coach keeps immediate decision context in working memory (current batter's form, recent bowlers deployed), but archives detailed delivery logs from earlier sessions in semantic storage, retrieving them only when pattern matching suggests relevance. When India faces England's reverse-swing bowling, the coach retrieves historical records of Edgbaston's (a similar venue) challenging conditions—this retrieval-augmented approach means the coach can reference millions of past experiences without holding them all in active focus. Temporal reasoning surfaces constantly: 'At this rate, we'll reach lunch 10 runs short of 200—I need to accelerate now but schedule more careful batting from 4pm onwards when the pitch deteriorates.' Distributed coordination becomes critical: the batting coach advises aggression, but the physio signals Rohit has fatigue accumulation and shouldn't chase every delivery—both agents must coordinate rather than contradict each other, perhaps agreeing Rohit attacks fast bowlers but conserves energy against spinners. When things fail—a batter gets out unexpectedly—the coach must replay the decision sequence, identifying whether the aggressive strategy was wrong (replan completely) or the execution was faulty (current strategy remains valid). The entire coaching effort is overhead: if the staff spends 30 minutes debating strategy for every 20-minute over, the overhead exceeds the value gained. This mirrors the internal mechanics of agents: task scheduling, memory management with RAG retrieval, temporal reasoning, distributed coordination, failure recovery, and overhead management all operate concurrently, requiring careful tuning of response thresholds, memory budgets, and computational reservations. The insight is that agents operating at production scale are not just executing tasks—they're managing intricate system-level concerns that directly impact real-time decision quality.",

sections.content: Real-World Application

AI agents and agentic workflows are deployed at massive scale across industry leaders. Google's Vertex AI and vertex-agent-builder power autonomous customer service systems handling millions of requests daily. Netflix uses hierarchical task-planning agents for content recommendation personalization across 230M subscribers, decomposing user preferences into sub-goals for genre, viewing history, and trending content. Uber's dispatch system employs multi-agent reinforcement learning with resource allocation constraints—matching 100M+ drivers to 50M+ daily rides while managing supply/demand imbalances. OpenAI's GPT-4 with function-calling enables enterprise workflow automation; Meta's Llama models power internal agentic systems for content moderation at Facebook scale. Airbnb implements state-management architectures for complex booking workflows involving inventory, pricing, cancellations, and regulatory compliance across 7M listings. When done wrong—insufficient failure recovery causes cascading booking failures (Airbnb 2021 outage), poor state management leads to inconsistent recommendations (Netflix's recommendations degradation), and inadequate constraint satisfaction creates deadlock in ride-matching (Uber surge pricing failures).

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A batting order decomposition assigns roles (openers face pace, middle-order handles spin, tail defends). State management tracks ball-by-ball context (score, wickets, overs). Resource allocation optimizes batting resources (who faces which bowler). Adaptive replanning switches strategy when early wickets fall. At international tournaments with 100M viewers, bad state tracking causes commentary errors; poor replanning costs matches when conditions shift.

items: Goal decomposition breaks complex tasks into hierarchical sub-goals; validate tree depth doesn't exceed system latency budgets.
State management must maintain consistency across distributed agents; use event sourcing or immutable snapshots for auditability.
Adaptive replanning detects failures early via sensor feedback; implement circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.
Resource constraints (compute, API quotas, memory) must be satisfied upfront; use constraint propagation, not greedy allocation.
Real-world agents fail silently; instrument observability (traces, metrics, alerts) on every critical path and fallback trigger.
Hierarchical task planning reduces token costs and latency; encode domain knowledge as task templates, not free-form prompts.
Test agentic workflows under adversarial conditions (network delays, missing data, quota exhaustion) before production deployment.

sections.content: You're building an AI agent for a cricket academy that schedules training sessions for 500 bowlers. The agent decomposes the goal into: (1) filter bowlers by skill level, (2) allocate 10 nets per time slot, (3) assign coaches. After assigning 350 bowlers, the system detects 200 additional bowlers were added overnight. The schedule is now invalid. What should the adaptive replanning mechanism do first?
items: Pause all assignments, re-run full decomposition with new constraints, then incrementally re-schedule only new bowlers into remaining slots.
Immediately assign new bowlers to any available slot without re-validating resource constraints.
Mark the schedule as failed and alert the academy manager to manually fix conflicts.
Delete previous assignments and rebuild from scratch using a different decomposition strategy.
sections.explanation: Option A is correct because it applies selective replanning: pausing prevents further invalid assignments, re-running decomposition incorporates new bowlers into the constraint model, and incremental re-scheduling preserves valid prior assignments (minimizing disruption). Option B violates resource constraints (nets exceed capacity), causing runtime failures. Option C abandons automation unnecessarily—intelligent replanning should recover. Option D wastes computational cost and disrupts 350 already-scheduled bowlers. This mirrors production systems (Netflix, Uber) that use delta-replanning for new data rather than full rebuilds.

sections.content: A recommendation agent for a cricket streaming platform maintains state: {user_id, watch_history[], preferred_formats[], last_updated}. A user simultaneously watches a match on mobile while updating their profile on web. Both requests trigger state updates, but the agent's state management uses simple in-memory updates without versioning. What failure occurs, and how should state management be redesigned?
items: Lost update: use optimistic locking or event sourcing; both requests increment a version counter, conflicts trigger merge logic or replay from immutable event log.
Deadlock: add mutual exclusion locks; serialize all state updates sequentially to prevent concurrent writes.
Cache coherence failure: flush all caches and reload state from disk on every update.
Race condition is acceptable; just log both updates and let the database eventual consistency resolve it later.
sections.explanation: Option A correctly identifies lost update anomaly: when concurrent writes bypass versioning, one update is silently overwritten. Optimistic locking (version counters) detects conflicts and triggers application-level merge or retry; event sourcing (immutable logs) replays transactions deterministically, ensuring consistency even with concurrent requests. Option B over-corrects with pessimistic locking, reducing throughput unnecessarily (problematic at Netflix/Airbnb scale). Option C flushes cache too aggressively, causing latency spikes. Option D is dangerous for e-commerce/booking systems—lost updates cause incorrect recommendations, booking double-allocations, and poor user experience. This pattern is used by Google Spanner, Netflix's state management, and Uber's CQRS architecture.

sections.content: How It Works Under the Hood

AI agents execute through a continuous perception-decision-action loop managed by a runtime engine. The agent maintains an internal state representation (observation space) that gets updated after each action. The decision-making process involves forward-chaining through the policy network or backward-chaining through task decomposition, depending on the architecture. Memory systems operate in layers: working memory holds immediate context (current goal, recent observations), episodic memory stores past interactions for learning, and semantic memory encodes domain knowledge. Execution flow follows a tick-based or event-driven model where the agent polls sensors, updates state, evaluates goal conditions against constraints, selects actions via value functions or probability distributions, and commits those actions to the environment. Performance characteristics depend on computational complexity of state transitions (often O(n²) for constraint checking), memory bandwidth for context retrieval, and latency of sensor feedback loops. Replanning triggers occur when predicted outcomes diverge from observed outcomes beyond a threshold, invoking recovery strategies that either modify subgoal sequences or reallocate resources.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Rohit Sharma captaining India during a Test match operates through continuous cycles. At each ball, Rohit (the agent) perceives the batsman's stance, field conditions, and bowler fatigue (perception). He maintains working memory of the match situation—current run rate, wickets lost, overs remaining—while his semantic memory recalls historical matchups and Bumrah's bowling patterns (memory layers). Rohit decides whether to keep Bumrah bowling or bring in Siraj based on expected outcomes and constraint satisfaction—fatigue limits, field balance, match situation (decision policy). He commits the action: "Bumrah, continue with short-pitched deliveries" (action execution). After each over, Rohit observes actual results—did the strategy work? Did the batsman adapt? (state update). If his prediction fails—say Kohli gets smashed for 16 runs—Rohit replans: maybe switch bowlers or adjust field placement (replanning). The match's perception-decision-action cycles, memory architecture, and adaptive replanning directly mirror how an AI agent operates: continuous sensing, internal state management, policy-driven decisions, and reactive recovery when reality diverges from expectation.

import heapq
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple
from enum import Enum
import time

class BowlerState(Enum):
    FRESH = 1
    FATIGUED = 2
    INJURED = 3

@dataclass
class MatchContext:
    """Working memory: immediate match state"""
    current_over: int
    runs_target: int
    wickets_lost: int
    bowler_fatigue: Dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)
    field_placement: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)

@dataclass
class BowlerProfile:
    """Semantic memory: historical bowler knowledge"""
    name: str
    avg_economy: float
    wicket_rate: float
    preferred_lengths: List[str]
    vs_batsman: Dict[str, float]  # effectiveness vs specific batsmen

class CricketAgent:
    """Agentic workflow for Test match captaincy"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.working_memory: MatchContext = None
        self.episodic_memory: List[Dict] = []  # Past decisions and outcomes
        self.semantic_memory: Dict[str, BowlerProfile] = {}
        self.policy_priority_queue = []  # Actions ranked by expected value
        self.replanning_threshold = 0.3  # Divergence tolerance
        
    def perceive(self, observation: Dict) -> MatchContext:
        """Update internal state from environment"""
        self.working_memory = MatchContext(
            current_over=observation['over'],
            runs_target=observation['target'],
            wickets_lost=observation['wickets'],
            bowler_fatigue=observation['bowler_fatigue'],
            field_placement=observation['field'],
            timestamp=time.time()
        )
        return self.working_memory
    
    def decide(self, goal: str) -> Tuple[str, float]:
        """Decision-making via expected value computation"""
        if goal == "contain_runs":
            candidates = self._rank_bowling_options()
            if candidates:
                best_action, confidence = heapq.heappop(candidates)
                return best_action, confidence
        return "defensive_field", 0.5
    
    def _rank_bowling_options(self) -> List[Tuple[str, float]]:
        """Backward-chain from goal to rank actions by constraint satisfaction"""
        actions = []
        for bowler_name, profile in self.semantic_memory.items():
            fatigue_penalty = self.working_memory.bowler_fatigue.get(bowler_name, 0)
            expected_value = profile.wicket_rate - (fatigue_penalty * 0.2)
            
            # Constraint: can't bowl if over-fatigued
            if fatigue_penalty < 0.8:
                actions.append((f"bowl_{bowler_name}", expected_value))
        
        heapq.heapify(actions)
        return actions
    
    def act(self, action: str) -> Dict:
        """Execute decision in environment"""
        self.episodic_memory.append({
            'action': action,
            'context': self.working_memory,
            'timestamp': time.time()
        })
        return {'status': 'executed', 'action': action}
    
    def replan(self, predicted_outcome: float, observed_outcome: float) -> bool:
        """Trigger replanning on divergence"""
        divergence = abs(predicted_outcome - observed_outcome)
        if divergence > self.replanning_threshold:
            print(f"⚠️ Divergence detected: {divergence:.2f}. Replanning...")
            # Query episodic memory for similar past situations
            recovery_actions = self._find_recovery_strategy()
            return True
        return False
    
    def _find_recovery_strategy(self) -> List[str]:
        """Fault recovery: search episodic memory for similar failures"""
        # Simplified: retrieve last 5 similar match states
        return [entry['action'] for entry in self.episodic_memory[-5:]]

# Usage
agent = CricketAgent()
agent.semantic_memory['bumrah'] = BowlerProfile(
    name='Jasprit Bumrah',
    avg_economy=2.8,
    wicket_rate=0.65,
    preferred_lengths=['yorker', 'short'],
    vs_batsman={'rohit': 0.3, 'kohli': 0.5}
)

observation = {
    'over': 45,
    'target': 280,
    'wickets': 2,
    'bowler_fatigue': {'bumrah': 0.6, 'siraj': 0.4},
    'field': ['slip', 'gully', 'mid_on']
}

agent.perceive(observation)
action, confidence = agent.decide('contain_runs')
agent.act(action)
agent.replan(predicted_outcome=2.1, observed_outcome=3.8)

Warning: State divergence in agentic systems can cascade rapidly if undetected. Replanning thresholds that are too high delay recovery; thresholds too low trigger excessive recomputation. Monitor the ratio of replanning events to total decision cycles—values >0.2 indicate systemic prediction errors or poorly calibrated models.

items: Runtime execution follows a continuous perception-decision-action loop with state updates after each cycle.
Memory operates in three layers: working (immediate context), episodic (past interactions), semantic (domain knowledge).
Forward-chaining uses value functions to rank actions; backward-chaining decomposes goals into subgoals and constraints.
Performance bottlenecks typically occur in state transition computation and constraint satisfaction (quadratic complexity).
Replanning thresholds detect divergence between predicted and observed outcomes; recovery invokes episodic memory or goal restructuring.
Sensor latency and feedback loops define the agent's responsiveness; tick-based systems sacrifice reactivity for computational predictability.

sections.content: In the code example, what mechanism triggers the captain's replanning?
items: When bowler fatigue exceeds 0.8
When predicted outcome diverges from observed outcome beyond the replanning_threshold
At the end of every over
When a wicket falls
sections.explanation: The replan() method explicitly checks: if divergence > replanning_threshold, it triggers recovery. This mirrors how a captain would adapt strategy only if reality significantly deviates from expectation—not on every minor event. The threshold (0.3) acts as a tolerance band; small prediction errors don't warrant costly replanning cycles.

sections.content: Common Patterns & Best Practices

Production agentic workflows employ three dominant patterns: the Supervisor pattern, where a central orchestrator manages multiple specialist agents and handles cross-agent communication—optimal for heterogeneous tasks but introduces latency and single points of failure; the Peer-to-Peer pattern, where agents collaborate directly without central coordination, reducing latency and improving fault tolerance but increasing complexity in state synchronization and conflict resolution; and the Hierarchical Planning pattern, organizing agents into layers where higher-level agents decompose goals into subgoals for lower-level agents, trading computational overhead for improved modularity and scalability. The Supervisor pattern dominates in customer service automation and content generation pipelines where task clarity is high; P2P excels in real-time multi-robot coordination and distributed game-playing scenarios where latency is critical; Hierarchical Planning proves essential in complex manufacturing and logistics systems with hundreds of subtasks. Cost trade-offs manifest as: Supervisor incurs 15-30% communication overhead but enables centralized monitoring; P2P reduces latency by 40-60% but requires sophisticated consensus mechanisms consuming 20-35% additional compute; Hierarchical adds planning overhead (10-25% CPU) but dramatically reduces state space explosion in complex domains. Selection depends on your bottleneck: communication-constrained environments favor P2P, CPU-constrained favor Supervisor, and scaling to 100+ coordinated tasks favors Hierarchical approaches.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The Supervisor pattern mirrors a captain making all tactical decisions—setting fields, rotating bowlers, calling DRS reviews from a central position. This works perfectly for Test cricket where time permits deliberation but fails in T20 where millisecond decisions matter. The Peer-to-Peer pattern resembles fielders communicating directly (slip fielders coordinating, bowler and keeper syncing without captain's constant input), reducing decision latency dramatically but requiring all fielders to understand the overall strategy. The Hierarchical pattern is like a cricket team's structure: captain (top agent) sets overall match strategy, bowlers' captain manages bowling rotations, batting captain (if different) manages batting order, and each batter (agent) executes within their assigned role. In high-pressure matches like IPL finals, teams use hybrid approaches—captain delegates heavily to specialist leaders (hierarchy) but opens channels for direct fielder communication (P2P) on crucial moments. Choosing between them depends on match format (your task complexity) and field conditions (your constraints): ODI cricket uses Supervisor heavily; T20 demands P2P responsiveness; Test cricket can afford Hierarchical planning overhead.

Warning: Creating agents without explicit communication contracts. Beginners often build agents that assume implicit understanding of message formats, leading to silent failures where Agent A sends batting_decision={"runs": 4} but Agent B expects {"runs_scored": 4, "balls_faced": 1}. This causes downstream agents to misinterpret state, leading to cascading failures. FIX: (1) Define JSON schemas for all inter-agent messages using Pydantic or JSON Schema before implementation; (2) Add message validation at every agent boundary using try-except with descriptive errors; (3) Implement a message broker (Redis/RabbitMQ) that enforces schema compliance; (4) Log all inter-agent communications for debugging. Example: Create a @dataclass for each message type (BattingDecision, FieldUpdate, BowlerRotation) and validate on receipt using isinstance() checks with fallback error handlers that notify the supervisor agent.

items: Supervisor pattern centralizes control but introduces communication bottlenecks—use for complex orchestration with clear task dependencies
Peer-to-Peer pattern minimizes latency through direct communication—essential for real-time reactive systems but requires sophisticated conflict resolution
Hierarchical pattern scales to complex multi-level tasks—optimal for manufacturing, logistics, and game-playing with 50+ coordinated agents
Always define explicit message schemas before building agent communication—prevents silent failures from format mismatches
Trade-off analysis: prioritize based on your bottleneck (latency, CPU, communication bandwidth, or task complexity)

sections.content: You're building an agentic system for real-time cricket match analytics with strict 100ms decision latency requirements. Bowler agents, field positioning agents, and fatigue prediction agents must coordinate continuously. Which pattern is most appropriate and why?
items: Supervisor pattern—central captain orchestrates all decisions for consistency
Peer-to-Peer pattern—direct communication between specialist agents minimizes latency; each agent caches field state independently
Hierarchical pattern—too many organizational layers will exceed latency budget
Monolithic agent—simplest implementation for real-time constraints
sections.explanation: Peer-to-Peer pattern is correct. Real-time cricket analytics with 100ms latency cannot tolerate the communication overhead of a Supervisor (adds 30-50ms round-trip delays through orchestrator). P2P allows bowler agents to push updates directly to field agents, reducing decision latency to 20-40ms. The cost is managing eventual consistency in cached field states, but this is acceptable for tactical updates. Supervisor pattern would add unacceptable delays; Hierarchical adds planning overhead; Monolithic prevents independent testing and reuse of specialist agents. In cricket terms: T20 cricket demands the coordination speed of fielders communicating directly, not waiting for captain's decision from 100m away.
